name: Tarmabar
description: The Tarmas were second only to the King, but had a history of subordination against the throne. Surprisingly, his seat of power was not a well-defended fortress, but rather a pleasant, open estate filled with dozens of relatives and servants. In the Third Age, Tarmabar became a secret refuge for the Northern Rangers. The ancient ruins were known to the Hobbits as Nob Hill.

name: Sunthra Unsar
description: Originally built under the name Mistalonde, the city appeared in records from the mid-Second Age until T.A. 1503, when Gordaig of Saerlann captured and renamed it Sunthra Unsar. The rebuilt city became the seat of power of the Saerlannish (Dynion) clan, and its inhabitants were almost entirely hillmen.

name: Eolburg
description: Buhr Ailgra is one of the few permanent colonies of the Eothraim, who follow the migratory movements of their flocks across the plains of Rhovanion. The village is a simple community with houses built of straw and peat, surrounded by a sturdy earthen fence.

name: Oatbarton
description: In the early days of the Shire, the region of Oatbarton was the site of an ancient Arthadan settlement known as Barrow. Oatbarton later became a Hobbit town and farming colony. The name was Bree-land slang meaning oats and barley sausage.

name: Nornhabar
description: The dwarven population varied considerably, having reached its peak in the Second Age. It was mainly abandoned before the War of the Last Alliance and, although never completely deserted, was largely dilapidated by T.A. 1643.

name: Frogmorton
description: A successful community still holding its open-air meetings on the hill south of the town. Its central location on the Great East Road made it an ideal location for regional gatherings. Frogmorton Market was held at the bottom of the hill.

name: Cair Andros
description: Cair Andros (S. Ship of Long Foam) was an island fortress on the River Anduin, roughly forty miles north of Osgiliath. Shaped like a great ship with a high rocky prow pointing north, it was fortified by Steward Túrin II to protect Anórien from enemy crossings. It served as a vital northern bulwark of Gondor against Mordor throughout the late Third Age.

name: Beldwin
description: Beldwin lay just south of the River Isen, near an ancient bridge still in use, built by the Dúnedain in ancient times. In the days of the Riddermark, it was renamed Fréaham and became the main settlement of Westmarch.

name: Minas Thonfalon
description: Ruined tower. Located in the middle of the marshy Swamp Coast, its foundations were slowly sinking into the waterlogged ground.

name: Annuminas
description: Ruined city. Once the first capital of Arnor, Annúminas stood on the southern shores of Lake Evendim. In its prime it glittered against the night sky, its festive torches visible across the Northern Kingdom. By the Third Age little remained but stone blocks and collapsed columns, a haunting and desolate place.

name: Cathiel
description: Ruined city. Cathiel was a settlement of Ethir Gwathló in Cardolan, about seventy-five miles inland from the mouths of the Gwathló. It had a population of roughly 4,000 in T.A. 250, declining through the Third Age and finally abandoned by T.A. 1640.

name: Calembel
description: The main town of Lamedon, Calembel (S. Greenham) was conveniently located east of the Fords of Ciril, on the road linking central Gondor with the mining communities of the southern White Mountains. People from across Lamedon and northern Mornan traded goods here; most ore was shipped directly to Linhir and Pelargir.

name: Bree
description: Bree is the largest settlement of Bree-land, a region that is peaceful, agricultural, and largely self-governing. Built around a large hill and protected by a formidable dyke topped with a thick hedge, it is one of the few places in Middle-earth where Men and Hobbits live side by side. Its central location on the Great East Road makes it a crossroads of trade and information.

name: Greenholm
description: A quiet outpost in the middle Third Age, home to a few Arthadan families coexisting with nearby Elves who observed the comings and goings of mortals on behalf of the lords of the Grey Havens. One of the Elves, Sinda Calenlinn, was an expert herbalist fond of fermented grains.

name: Enerond
description: Ruined city. Enerond was a township of Brethyar in Arthedain, on the east bank of the Lune River. It had a population of about 5,100 in T.A. 250, declining through the Third Age until abandoned before its end.

name: Ethring
description: Ethring (S. Cold Spring) was established during the Gondorian conquest on the eastern bank of the Ringló at the junction of the Rathon Arat. Its name derived from the icy mountain streams nearby. Ethring became a large market town at harvest time, serving travelers along the Gondor Road, with mining and blacksmithing as subsidiary industries.

name: Fornost Erain
description: Located on a steep rise on the southern slopes of the North Downs, Fornost served as both fortress and town — the royal seat of Arthedain after the sack of Annúminas in T.A. 1409. A somber mood pervaded the court as the Dúnedain stood ever ready to face another assault from the Witch-King. The wisest scholars and the largest library of the Northern Kingdoms were found here.

name: Harlond
description: Harlond was a more hospitable Elven harbour than Forlond or Mithlond, a place of simple pleasures rather than great deeds. Nearly invisible from the sea, it lay sheltered behind a protective promontory. Few large swan-ships departed from its docks, though many small elven pleasure craft anchored here before setting sail for the wider world.

name: Eithel Thurin
description: Eithel Túrin (S. The Well of Túrin) was a quiet settlement of several hundred souls, situated halfway between Methir and Barad Harn. It was founded in T.A. 932 by Gondorian veterans wounded in the wars against the Haruze and Umbarians.

name: Minas Arthor
description: Minas Arthor, once the capital of Arnach, was situated at the crossings of the Erui, where a settlement had existed since the earliest chronicles. Founded at the beginning of the Third Age, it grew into the center of a prosperous agricultural district.

name: Bar Astirian
description: Astorion Manor (S. Bâr Astorion) was the former home of House Astorion, a noble Gondorian family. It was situated on the outskirts of the Entwash Mouths, north of Min-Rimmon, where the family held extensive lands.

name: Ossarnen
description: Setmaenen, known as Ossarnen to the Dúnedain and Gondorians, was a small mining town in the province of Lamedon and the site of an ancient sacred place of the Mountain Men. The word Ossarnen alludes to the Setmaenen Stones, a pre-Númenórean ceremonial complex of megalithic structures outside the town.

name: Gram
description: The Witch-King's servants erected this mountain fortress on the southern branch of the Misty Mountains forming the boundary of Nan Angmar. Gram was the most formidable Orc stronghold west of the mountains; no man had ever escaped its depths to give a coherent account of its interior.

name: Imladris (Rivendell)
race: Noldor Elves, Silvan Elves, Sindar Elves, Half-Elves, Dúnedain
description: Rivendell (S. Imladris) was a refuge in the valley of the Bruinen, protected by the power of Elrond Half-elven and Vilya, the Ring of Air. Evil could not enter its boundaries; even the Nazgûl dared only to prowl its borders. The refuge offered counsel, healing, and rest to all free peoples who found their way to its hidden gates.

name: Tharbad
description: Since the fall of King Ostoher in T.A. 1410, the ancient Númenórean port of Tharbad had been losing the glory it once possessed. Still the largest city in Eriador, it was riven by petty rivalries among the surviving princes of Cardolan, guild conflicts, and a growing criminal class. Only its role as the main commercial hub north of Gondor kept the city from self-destruction.

name: Minas Ongoth
description: Ruined city.

name: Athrad Poros
description: The ford settlement of Athrad Poros was an important stop on the land trade route south. It began as a military garrison protecting the border between the nascent Kingdom-in-Exile and the Umbarean sphere of influence. When the Harnen Corridor opened, Athrad Poros became a critical juncture for trade with Near Harad and Khand.

name: Rood
description: Rood (originally Ruaduin), once the largest city of Nan Turnath, was a hum of commerce where the King's Road met the Baranduin. By the late Third Age it suffered severely from plague and the incompetence of its feudal lord. Many of its stone houses stood abandoned and decaying, while much of the surviving population had fled to Fornost.

name: Fangoth
description: Ruined city.

name: Minas Tirith
description: Originally called Minas Anor (S. Tower of the Sun), Minas Tirith was built at the foot of Mount Mindolluin in the Ered Nimrais. The city rose in seven concentric tiers of white stone, each gate offset from the last, and the White Tower housed the last Palantír in Gondor's keeping. Together with Osgiliath and Minas Ithil, it formed the core of Gondor.

name: Mithlond
description: Mithlond (S. Grey Havens) was the chief harbour of Lindon, ruled directly by Círdan the Shipwright and his council. Fast elven ships crossed the Gulf of Lhûn regularly; at night their lanterns made the waters dance with brilliant light. An open city of magic and beauty, Mithlond welcomed honest travelers, though its Elven welcome could seem cold to those unfamiliar with their customs.

name: Linhir
description: Linhir (S. River Fords) was a prosperous and busy port, its ancient Númenórean walls enclosing a city that had been an important urban center for millennia. A large population of unsavory characters preyed upon the weak and gullible amid the bustle of river trade.

name: Bar-i-Dinuviel
description: Bar-i-Dinuviel (S. Home of the Nightingale; Os. Mbar Tinduvela) was a fishing village on the coast of Dor Tinuviel.

name: Edoras
description: Edoras (R. The Courts) was the capital of Rohan, built upon an ancient hill site at the northern edge of the White Mountains. The great hall Meduseld crowned the hill, and the kings of the Mark were buried in twin rows of barrows outside the main gate. The surrounding treeless plains gave its lookouts a commanding view of all approaches.

name: Firaen
description: Ruined city.

name: Litash
description: Litash served as a training center for priests of the Dark Religion and as the primary base for tax collection in eastern Angmar. It was one of the Witch-King's chief instruments for controlling the eastern reaches of his domain.

name: Lond Daer
description: Ruined city. Established as Vinyalondë by Tar-Aldarion in the 9th century of the Second Age, later known as Lond Daer Enedh, it was a great Númenórean colonial port. It was devastated by the cataclysmic tsunami of S.A. 3319 that accompanied the Downfall of Númenor.

name: Woodmen Village
description: A small village of the Woodmen in the Upper Anduin Vales, partially built on large platforms in the trees in the Elven fashion, reflecting the friendly relations between the Woodmen and the nearby Wood-Elves.

name: Arailt
description: Arailt Hold was the ancient seat of the Dunlending Arailt clan. By the time of the War of the Ring it had long been abandoned; the Rohirrim had established a scouting camp above the ruins and nearby caves.

name: Cerin Amroth
description: Cerin Amroth (S. Hill of Amroth) was the wooded hill that served as the home and court of King Amroth of Lórien during the Third Age. Even after his departure, the hill retained a timeless quality, fragrant with elanor and niphredil.

name: Fennas Drunnin
description: A Council directed most affairs in Fennas Drúnin, a town that depended on local farms and vineyards for its prosperity. Some Hobbits lived nearby but generally avoided contact with the townspeople. Far from the main roads of Cardolan, it was a good base for anyone wishing to explore southern Rhudaur or the Elven ruins of Eregion.

name: Herubar Gular
description: Ruined castle. Originally a Broadbeam Dwarf camp, later fortified by the Númenóreans and then used as a Dúnadan lordly mansion after the founding of Arnor. Abandoned during the wars following Rhudaur's separatism, it became known as Herubar Gûlar (Manor of the Sorcerer) and was later used as a hideout by the Witch-King's agent Ar-Gûlar.

name: Eredimar
description: Eredimar (S. Mountain Home) lay at the foot of the Ered Luin within the valley of Imladorn (S. The Wooded Valley). Despite housing fewer than 100 people, it was remarkable for its cultural diversity — Hobbits, Dwarves, Elves, and Men had all settled here at various times. Sulfur springs nearby gave Eredimar renown as a place of healing, and three well-maintained lodges welcomed travelers.

name: Filimer
description: Ruined city.

name: Bar Irlossiel
description: Ruined castle. Barad Perinluoss, commonly known as the Silent Castle, was an ancient Elven refuge in southern Sîrgala — an independent community of Wood-Elves and Arthadan religious refugees who pledged fealty to a half-Elf known as Iralussiel the Tranquil. Iralussiel died mysteriously around the 11th or 12th century T.A., and the shelter became eerily peaceful thereafter.

name: Nan i Naugrim
description: The greatest mine of Nan-i-Naugrim and the capital of the Broadbeam Dwarves since the Second Age. By the mid-Third Age, Edennogrod — the largely ruined Khazad above Nan-i-Naugrim — stood as a shadow of its former self. At the end of the Third Age it was ruled by the feudal lord Grár, who held the honorary title Mountain King among the Shire-folk.

name: Minas Girithlin
description: The castle had withstood many sieges, as evidenced by the numerous scars left by fire and siege engines on its exterior walls.

name: Bar Adorn
description: Bar Adorn was a settlement on the River Adorn near the western borders of Rohan, serving as a minor trading post between the Rohirrim and the Dunlendings of the region.

name: Saerthondost
description: Saerthondost was one of two Dúnedain strongholds protecting Pinnath Gelin and Nan Lefnui from the wilds of Hairaverkien. Located on the north bank of the Lefnui where the river turned sharply northwest, it was named for the nearby swamps rich in shepherd's herbs. A lighthouse tower linked it to its sister fortress Pinnornost to the northeast; it was founded in T.A. 823 and abandoned in T.A. 2157.

name: Cargash
description: Cargash was designed to warn the Witch-King of approaching intruders and to serve as a base for patrols into the No-Man's-Land of En Udanoriath between Angmar and Arthedain. Regular sorties launched from Cargash kept the frontier under constant surveillance.

name: Minas Ithil
description: Minas Ithil (S. Tower of the Moon) was a Gondorian city protected by a deep valley in the Ephel Dúath and the largest urban center east of the Anduin. Known as the intellectual and spiritual capital of Gondor, it was a refuge for artists, scholars, and seekers of tolerance. Its dramatic architecture was unrivaled in the kingdom.

name: Michel Delving
description: Michel Delving was the chief town and administrative center of the Shire, located in the White Downs of Westfarthing. It was the seat of the Mayor of the Shire and housed the Mathom-house, a museum of Hobbit curiosities.

name: Vidusbaurg
description: Buhr Widu, situated near the small Forest Lake, retained some of the royal splendor of the days of Vidugavia. The Grammuz princes who ruled it after the Plague lacked the strength to restore its former glory. Like most Northman villages on the plains, it consisted of long wooden and thatched houses defended by an earthen wall and sturdy wooden towers.

name: Morkai
description: Barad Morkai rose atop a granite promontory overlooking the Way of Angmar. The fortress comprised thick walls and square bastions, built to withstand the most powerful siege engines of the age.

name: Faranel
description: Ruined city.

name: Tilmendir
description: Tilmendir monitored traffic moving between Westmarch and Anfalas, and projected Gondorian authority over the local Dunnish tribes.

name: Guinnion
description: Ruined village.

name: Bodroth
description: Bodroth was a city of Eketyar in Arthedain, situated on the northern shores of Lake Evendim about forty miles north of Annúminas. It had a population of roughly 4,900 in T.A. 250, but declined through the Third Age and was eventually abandoned, though it survived the fall of the last King by several generations.

name: Culwic
description: Culwic, originally known as Himtil or Himobel (S. Chill Village) and later as Colwich, was a small town in western Arthedain on the Little Lune, just north of the borders of Dor Lelvin and Lindon. Its mixed population of Men and Dwarves supported the dwarven settlements to the north through industry and agriculture.

name: Cirith Dudhrandir
description: Cirith Dudhrandir was the only major mountain pass through the Belfalas Peninsula and the only one with a paved road, making it strategically vital for Gondor's southern defenses. At 5,800 feet and over sixty miles east to west, it was guarded by the fortress of Naur Amrûn. The pass was named in S.A. 3400 when Edhelion of Eldalonde received Belfalas as a fief.

name: Forlond
description: Forlond, the Elven harbour of Forlindon, lay hidden in a steep fjord whose sheltered waters protected the Elven fleet from the storms of the Belegaer. Less populous than the nearby Grey Havens, it was nonetheless an important coastal centre where Elves built beautiful ships destined to carry them to Aman.

name: Rildolach
description: Ruined city.

name: Iant Methed
description: Iant Methed (The Last Bridge) was built of stone with three great arches at the height of Arnorian power in T.A. 320. In the petty wars of Rhudaur the crossing was generally considered neutral ground. The Last Inn, long run by the Grumm family, stood nearby; just northwest lay the ruins of Dol Elerilde, stronghold of an old lord of Rhudaur.

name: Laur Dunnen
description: Larach Duhnnan lay on territory of Clan Feargan but the flow of foreign traffic made it a neutral ground for inter-tribal discussions and trade. It had no walls, relying on the nearby Feargan fortress for defense, and sat at one of the few accessible crossings of the Dunstrem, for which the clan lords charged a modest toll.

name: Talsir
description: Talsir was one of the free cities of Cardolan on the west bank of the Gwathló, about 170 miles downstream from Tharbad. With a population of roughly 4,300 in T.A. 250, it was an important river stop and one of the few settlements of ancient Minhiriath to survive into the late Third Age.

name: Framsburg
description: Wraecaburg, though not directly under the Witch-King's rule, suffered frequent attacks and harbored many unsavory characters from outside Rhovanion. Its men occasionally traded with kinsmen at Maethelburg, but relations grew increasingly suspicious over time.

name: Amon Sul
description: Ruined tower. From this vantage point one had an excellent view of the Great East Road heading toward Rhudaur, making it a strategic focus contested by Arthedain and Cardolan for generations. The Tower of Amon Sûl once housed a Palantír, moved to Fornost after Angmar's invasion in T.A. 1409.

name: Caras Galadhon
description: A fortified and beautiful city at the heart of Lórien, Caras Galadhon was an interconnected network of flets (S. Telain) built upon the great trunks of mallorn trees. Galadriel herself devised its plan, foreseeing the dangers her kingdom would face once Sauron forged the One Ring.

name: Endrist
description: Ruined city.

name: Gamwich
description: Gamwich (W. Galabas, meaning Play Place) was a small village in the Westfarthing of the Shire, notable as the ancestral home of the Gamgee family.

name: Lorilad
description: Lórilad was a fortified village of Lebennin, situated in the Ethir Anduin roughly seventy-five miles downstream from Pelargir, halfway between Pelargir and the western cape of Lebennin.

name: Larad
description: Ruined city.

name: Sackville
description: Originally Sairinan (S. Valley of the Wizards), an ancient Eriadoran ruin repopulated by Northmen after T.A. 1412, the settlement became Saccburh before taking on the Hobbit name Sackville. Southfarthing's second-largest town, it sat amid wheat fields, orchards, and terraced vineyards. Sackville wine and pipeweed reached as far as Saerlann and Dale.

name: Amon Din
description: Amon Din was the easternmost of the seven beacon towers of Gondor. Its fire signal alerted Minas Anor of any attack on the northern borders, initiating the chain of beacons westward across Gondor.

name: Buzdum Mazar
description: The greatest mine of Nan-i-Naugrim and the capital of the Broadbeam Dwarves since the Second Age. By the mid-Third Age, Edennogrod — the largely ruined Khazad above Nan-i-Naugrim — dominated the area. At the end of the Third Age it was governed by the feudal lord Grár, who bore the honorary title Mountain King among the Shire-folk.

name: Minas Brethil
description: Minas Brethil (S. Silver Birch Tower), capital of Celossien, was situated in the upper Serni valley and served as an agricultural center between the Glinduin and Serni rivers. After the Kin-strife it held some 4,000 inhabitants, a number halved by the Great Plague and only slowly recovered thereafter.

name: Cithrenor
description: Ruined town.

name: Maegoth
description: Ruined city.

name: Enerond
description: Ruined city.

name: Fanduin
description: Fanduin had a fairly large population of 4,100 in T.A. 250, but its numbers declined through the middle Third Age and the settlement was eventually abandoned before the end of the Third Age.

name: Pelargir
description: Pelargir (S. Garth of Royal Ships) was founded by the Faithful of Númenor in S.A. 2350 and became the chief harbour and oldest city of Gondor, built on the Anduin at the mouth of the Sirith. It served as the base of Gondor's great fleet and was the kingdom's primary port of trade, second city only to Minas Tirith.

name: Metraith
description: A town at the junction of the Redway and North Road; after the partition of Arnor the Cardolan king used it as a summer retreat called Thalion. The princes of Dol Tinereb dwelt in a great ruined mansion here. As one of the few viable towns between Bree and Tharbad, Metraith was an important caravan supply center.

name: Endil
description: Endil (S. Midpoint) was a coastal lighthouse tower maintained by the Prince of Dor-en-Ernil to alert Lond Ernil of any Corsair landing on the shores of Belfalas. Situated on a solitary islet a mile offshore, it stood at the midpoint between Metrast and Dol Amroth and served as the warden's residence for the coastal defense system.

name: Archet
description: Archet comprised about thirty-five stone houses among rolling cultivated hills. The village was known for an unusual cheese produced by goats feeding on the rare Arinyalasse (Q. Morning-petal) wildflower that grew only in the immediate vicinity. Located three miles from Bree in fertile Bree-land.

name: Gundabad
description: Mount Gundabad was a sacred site of the Dwarves and an important enclave in the northern Misty Mountains. It was seized by Orcs multiple times and eventually became the capital of the northern Orc-realm, a constant threat to the free peoples of the north.

name: Caras Celairnen
description: Situated on a rocky rise of dry land across the marshy confluence of the Uialduin and Lhûn rivers, Caras Celairnen was an ancient site continuously inhabited since the early Second Age.

name: Nadior
description: Ruined city. Nadior was a settlement of Ethir Gwathló in Cardolan, in a small bay on the Minhiriath coast about seventy miles west of the Gwathló mouths. It had a population of roughly 4,900 in T.A. 250, declining through the mid-Third Age until finally abandoned by T.A. 1640.

name: Fanuilond
description: The port of Fanuilond in southern Gondor was often the last stop for coastal traders heading south. Near the islands and sandbars of the Mouths of Anduin, it was a damp but lively place popular with sailors for the quality of the local fruit and wine. The Tipsy Triton, a cheerful inn near the docks, served some of the best wines in the region.

name: Tirith Nindir
description: An ancient Gondorian fortress built to protect and guard the approaches to the Dagorlad plain.

name: Lond Angren
description: Lond Angren (S. Iron Port) began as a small Númenórean outpost of the Second Age and grew into a city and lordship controlling western Enedwaith near the mouth of the River Angren.

name: Dunlostir
description: Dunlostir was a regional grain-trading center. Twice a year, harvest fairs transformed the normally quiet community into a vibrant and cosmopolitan hub of commerce.

name: Onodrith
description: Onodrith, later known as Entrift or Stowburg, was a staging post for most river traffic to the northern and western provinces of Gondor. Large granaries along the river stored grain reserves for the King of Gondor and Rohan. The ferry handled heavy traffic, though high tolls drove many travelers to seek the Iach Onodlo ford some twenty miles upstream.

name: Cor Angaladh
description: A ritual burial ground, now long disused. Once one of the most sacred sites of the Northmen, this place is now shadowed by the darkness of Mirkwood. Men of the north avoid it, believing it to be cursed.

name: Dale
description: Dale (Del. Dalur; R. Dala) was a prosperous city of the Northmen, nestled in the sheltered valley below Erebor. Famed for its skilled craftsmen and fine goods, it traded widely via the River Celduin to markets in Rhûn and Gondor. The city rested in the shadow of the Lonely Mountain and served as a base for those wishing to explore the Grey Mountains.

name: Rhosgobel
description: Rhosgobel was the rustic woodland home of Radagast the Brown, set within the great branches of an ancient oak at the southern edge of Mirkwood. It served as a place of healing and herb-lore, a refuge in the shadow of Dol Guldur, and a watch-post for observing the Necromancer's movements. Radagast tended one of the greatest herb-gardens in Middle-earth.

name: Esgaroth
description: Esgaroth (Lake-town) was built upon great wooden platforms over the Long Lake, connected to shore by a single bridge. Its citizens elected a new Master every two years, but the true master of Esgaroth was commerce — most trade routes of the region passed through it. The Northmen of Lake-town were notably cosmopolitan for their isolation.

name: Tumnogoth Iaur
description: The Dwarves of Tumnogath were poor and secretive. Many of their young had emigrated to the distant Iron Hills, and the community stagnated over a few generations. The remaining Dwarven population consisted largely of treasure-seekers and travelling tinkers.

name: Carrock
description: Carrock was a great rocky islet in the Anduin, between Iach Iaur and Maethelburg, held in deep spiritual reverence by the Beornings who dwelt nearby. The Anduin was shallow here, allowing a ford near the Carrock, though the swift current made the crossing genuinely dangerous.

name: Osgiliath
description: Osgiliath (S. Citadel of Stars) was the ancient capital of Gondor, spanning both banks of the Anduin midway between Minas Anor and Minas Ithil. At its height the city boasted sea-going quays, a great stone bridge lined with houses and towers, and the Dome of Stars which housed the largest of the seven Palantíri. Its decline accelerated after the Kin-strife and the Great Plague.

name: Tuckborough
description: Tuckborough was the ancestral seat of the Took family, located in the Green Hill Country of Westfarthing. The surrounding area included the market center of Whitwell, the woodcraft village of Tuckburrow, and Dolwich, which supplied timber and granite to the southern Farthings.

name: Nothva Rhaglaw
description: Nothva Rhaglaw (Du. Regent's Refuge) was the only permanent city of the Mountain-Men west of the Hoarwell, serving as a commercial center and source of local justice. The semi-nomadic movements of the hillmen caused its population to vary widely by season. It was an important waypoint for merchants carrying goods between Cardolan and Angmar.

name: Cameth Brin
description: Cameth Brin served as the seat of the hillman king Broggha. The citadel, built into the Twisted Hill with a tower and tunnel system constructed by the Dúnedain of Arnor, was a near-impregnable stronghold. It was one of the few permanent settlements of the semi-migratory hillmen and the main base for raids against Arthedain and Cardolan.

name: Faemar
description: Faemar was a village set midway along the long, narrow Taur Galen, at a ford crossing the river. The steep valley downstream made farming impossible; villagers subsisted on wild game, berries, herbs, nuts, and wood for charcoal.

name: Ambon Noirinyaron
description: Ruined mansion.

name: Dol Amroth
description: Dol Amroth (S. Hill of Amroth) was a rocky promontory on the western coast of Belfalas, named after the Sindarin lord Amroth son of Amdír, who founded Tirith Aear on its cliffs in the mid-Second Age. It encompassed the Númenórean castle of Ost-en-Ernil and the harbour town of Lond Ernil. Dol Amroth guarded one of Gondor's safest harbours and boasted the kingdom's largest fleet outside Pelargir.

name: Little Delving
description: Located in the White Downs, Little Delving had a higher proportion of Smials — the traditional Hobbit hillside dwelling — than many lowland settlements where tunnelling was impractical.

name: Stock
description: The people of Stock maintained a careful relationship with the surrounding Woody End, coppicing trees selectively and gathering apples, acorns, and nuts for baking. The fertile soil of the Baranduin valley produced some of the finest mushrooms in the Shire.

name: Bar-en-Dinnen
description: Bar-en-Dinnen (S. Dwelling on the Dinnen) was a fishing village and river-trading town in southern Ithilien, about sixty miles south of Minas Tirith, at the junction of the Tinnen and Anduin rivers. As the only significant settlement in Harithilien north of Hyarpende, it was an important hub for river commerce between Ithilien and Lebennin.

name: Mirolos
description: Mirolos (S. Jewel-Dream; also Londalos) was a Noldor settlement founded by followers of the Sons of Fëanor in the first decade of the Second Age, the largest community in the Hûb Conath region. Founded by Maglor before his disappearance, it was burned during the Long Winter when a northern dragon entered the area.

name: Annon Baran
description: Annon Baran was a dangerous open city where Orcs, Beffraen, River-men, Dúnedain, and even Corsairs from distant Umbar rubbed shoulders. For Hobbits looking southwest from the Shire, it represented the edge of the known world.

name: Forondir
description: Ruined city.

name: Hobbiton
description: Hobbiton was a prominent Hobbit village in the Westfarthing of the Shire, north of the Great East Road. It was home to Bag End on the Hill — the residence of Bilbo Baggins and later Frodo Baggins.

name: Glanhir
description: Glanhir (S. Between the Streams) was a city of Lamedon at the confluence of the Morthond and Ringló rivers, thirty miles from the sea. Its position made it the chief economic gateway to eastern Anfalas, the Blackroot Vale, and the Lamedon valley.

name: Amrelor
description: Ruined city.

name: Dol Guldur
description: Dol Guldur (S. Hill of Dark Sorcery; Orc. Nazburz; Rh. Naquath Hlain) was the great fortress of Sauron in southern Mirkwood and capital of the Necromancer's realm. Built within a dead volcanic hill, it cast its shadow over the whole of Mirkwood throughout the Third Age.

name: Lond Thalos
description: Lond Thalos (S. Port of Thalos) served as both a medical and herbal community and as a timber port supplying wood to the shipyards of the Grey Havens.

name: Scary
description: Located in the rugged country of Eastfarthing, Scary and its nearby quarries supplied most of the tin, copper, and granite used in the Shire. The village name derived literally from the scars left by quarrying and mining.

name: Hornburg
description: The Hornburg (R. Súthburg; Gon. Ostiras) was a great fortress of Rohan built into the cleft of Helm's Deep, consisting of a robust tower atop a great rock spur with a thick wall stretching across the mouth of the valley. It was the last refuge of the Westfold in times of war.

name: Elostirion
description: Elostirion was the tallest of the three White Towers on the Emyn Beraid (Tower Hills). It housed a Palantír that looked only westward toward the Undying Lands, keeping a constant vigil across the Sundering Sea.

name: Shedun
description: The broad plains below Shedun served as training ground and muster point for the Witch-King's Orc-hordes. Skirmishes between Orcs and the castle's human garrison were frequent, though the Witch-King's iron discipline kept the forces ready for deployment.

name: Moria
description: Khazad-dûm (Kh. Dwarven Mansion; S. Hadhodrond; W. Dwarrowdelf), also known as Moria (S. Black Chasm), was the greatest underground realm of the Dwarves, founded in the early First Age beneath the Misty Mountains. Seven great levels stretched across the range beneath Caradhras, Fanuidhol, and Celebdil. In the early Second Age the discovery of mithril drew Dwarves from the Blue Mountains; at its height Khazad-dûm was the mightiest Dwarven kingdom in Middle-earth.

name: Eruimar
description: Eruimar (S. Lonely Home) sat at the foot of the Ramluin (S. Blue Wall), below the easternmost point where the Ered Luin foothills gave way to rocky cliffs. Despite its remote setting, distance rather than forest or enchantment created its isolation. The village held no more than thirty-five households; founded by the Dúnedain, most of its roughly 150 residents were River-men or Northmen by the mid-Third Age.

name: Halifirien
description: Halifirien (S. Amon Anwar, Sacred Hill) stood on the border of Anórien and Calenardhon. Sacred since the Second Age as Eilenaer (Dn. Holy Hill) to the Men of the White Mountains, it later became the site of the Tomb of Elendil raised by Isildur. After Steward Cirion swore the Oath of Eorl here, the lands passed to the Eorlingas.

name: Derwath
description: Derwath (Eo. Hardscadu) was the largest city of Westmarch, occupying the site of an ancient Daen Coentis settlement. It grew into the chief commercial and agricultural center of the region, with several mills and a busy market. After the Great Plague it struggled to replace lost craftsmen; a park-lined southern quarter contrasted with a poorer northern district housing migrant workers.

name: Annon Wain
description: Annon Wain, in the upper Lefnui valley, was a local market center. To the north lay extensive mines whose ore was shipped downriver to Annúlond, while nearby firs from the Ered Nimrais supplied Gondor's naval yards. The town itself was modest — merchant houses, simple dwellings, and riverside warehouses — with a population of roughly 2,500.

name: Thoronbar
description: Ruined village.

name: Cerin Balgrist
description: Ruined city.

name: Cum Arelaid
description: Ruins. An ancient burial complex of pre-Númenórean tumuli and stone-lined graves, sacred to the early peoples of the region.

name: Ethraid Engrin
description: The Fords of Isen were the main crossing point for those traversing the Gap of Rohan, with no bridge spanning the powerful river. The Rohirrim patrolled the area as their primary western gateway; Dunlending hillmen often seized the fords and demanded tolls. Three rows of broad flat stones crossed the waters, with graded horse-fords between them.

name: Annodrun
description: Ruined city.

name: Melburg
description: Maethelburg (Meeting Fort) controlled virtually all trade through the upper Anduin valley and produced large quantities of tin from the lower Hithaeglir mines. Travelers heading west stocked up before crossing the Misty Mountains; those arriving from the east rested here. Built on an artificial mound behind a massive timber palisade, it harbored an unusually high number of spies due to its proximity to Angmar and the Orc-holds of Gundabad.

name: Iach Sarn
description: Iach Sarn clustered about the base of a limestone hill, surrounded by small farms and hardwood groves. On the south bank, just downstream of the main road, lay the ruins of an older village of earth-and-stone construction. Weathered steps led down a rubble embankment to the riverside docks, lined with unpainted ferries and fishing boats.

name: Carn Dum
description: Carn Dûm (S. Red Vale) was the dark capital of Angmar and the seat of the Witch-King. Built in a deep valley of the far north, it was a city of sorcery and terror — the source of every assault upon the kingdoms of Arnor throughout the Third Age.
